title: New Prime Minister, Same Problem
gist: Matthijs argues Brexit's deferred economic bill is now coming due under Labour: with global trade rules broken and rates high, the UK has no shock absorbers, gilt yields trade at large premiums to France and Germany, and the only escape is a grand bargain trading UK military power for EU single-market access, facing a political trilemma the new PM Andy Burnham cannot fully solve.

insights.claim: Brexit's non-tariff barriers raised the cost of trading with Europe by roughly four percent of long-run UK GDP per the Office for Budget Responsibility, a permanent income loss of about $132 billion a year, just under $4,650 per average household.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: Quantifies a permanent structural drag on UK growth that no fiscal tweak can offset, the macro backdrop for sterling and gilts.
insights.quote: a permanent reduction in national income to the order of $132 billion a year, just under $4,650 for the average household
insights.locator: WHAT HAS GONE / 'The decision to leave that arrangement'

insights.claim: By end-2023 UK real per capita GDP was roughly 28 percent below what 1955-2008 growth trends predicted, with Brexit worsening pre-existing weak productivity, underinvestment, and post-2008 austerity rather than being the sole cause.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: Frames the depth of UK underperformance; Brexit foreclosed the easiest routes back to growth.
insights.quote: British real per capita GDP was roughly 28 percent below what growth trends between 1955 and 2008 predicted
insights.locator: WHAT HAS GONE / 'As the British physicist Richard Jones has noted'

insights.claim: The Brexit critics were not wrong, just early: resilient (low-paid, low-productivity) UK employment masked the costs until the breakdown of the rules-based trading order and Trump's tariffs removed the cover.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: The deferral period is over; the bill is arriving precisely as global shock absorbers disappear.
insights.quote: They had not been wrong. They had just been early.
insights.locator: WHAT HAS GONE / 'For most of the post-referendum period'

insights.claim: UK ten-year yields trade at a premium of roughly 115 basis points over comparable French bonds and 185 over German ones, reflecting not London's public finances (similar to Paris and Berlin) but weaker relative growth prospects and diminished shock-absorbing capacity.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: The market is pricing a structural growth/credibility gap that only an EU realignment or harsh austerity can close, a directional view on gilt spreads.
insights.quote: Its ten-year yields now trade at a premium of roughly 115 basis points over comparable French bonds and 185 over German ones
insights.locator: WHAT HAS GONE / 'But the United Kingdom has consistently underperformed'

insights.claim: Rising UK gilt yields are not primarily due to Labour's fiscal decisions, since Reeves has been more disciplined than her Conservative predecessors, but reflect global rates plus a UK-specific growth penalty.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: Fiscal discipline alone will not compress the spread; the binding constraint is growth, which points to the EU relationship.
insights.locator: WHAT HAS GONE / 'This is not primarily the result of Labour's fiscal decisions'

insights.claim: Labour trails Nigel Farage's Reform UK by nearly ten points in most national polls, a swing of roughly 30 points from 2024 when Reform ran ~20 points below Labour, prompting Starmer to announce his resignation on June 22.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: UK political risk is elevated and pro-Brexit populism is ascendant, complicating any EU rapprochement.
insights.quote: Labour has been trailing Nigel Farage's insurgent Reform UK party in most national polls by almost ten points

insights.claim: Public opinion has shifted decisively: 56 percent of Britons now say leaving the EU was wrong, and roughly four in five Labour voters support opening customs-union negotiations, though there is no consensus for full rejoining.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: The political ceiling on EU re-engagement has risen sharply; the debate is now about how close, not whether.
insights.quote: 56 percent of Britons said it was wrong to leave

themes.theme: The trilemma and the grand bargain

insights.claim: The new PM faces a trilemma, able to pick only two of three: legitimacy of the Brexit settlement, bond-market credibility via a growth strategy, and unity of Labour's electoral coalition.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: A clean mental model for UK policy paralysis; any path forward sacrifices one objective, with predictable market or political consequences.
insights.quote: The new prime minister can have any two of three objectives
insights.locator: TRIPLE TROUBLE / 'Burnham, who is now overwhelmingly likely'

insights.claim: The proposed escape is a grand bargain: the UK offers the EU binding access to its military and intelligence capabilities by treaty in return for durable single-market and customs access, with opt-outs (no euro, no unconditional free movement) to make it politically saleable.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: The actionable scenario that would re-rate UK growth and compress gilt spreads; watch for any move toward a UK-EU security-for-trade treaty.
insights.quote: the United Kingdom offers the EU access to its substantial military and intelligence capabilities in return for durable access to the single market
insights.locator: TRIPLE TROUBLE / 'If there is an exit from the trilemma'

insights.claim: The bargain is more feasible now because a Trump-jolted Europe is rearming, with the EU disbursing a $175 billion program of first-ever collective defense borrowing, and France-UK 'coalitions of the willing' already anchoring Ukraine's defense after US aid collapsed from over $19 billion in 2024 to a token $400 million in 2026.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: Europe's new defense urgency gives the UK pricing power for its military contributions, the leverage that makes the trade deal plausible.
insights.quote: the EU began disbursing a $175 billion rearmament program, borrowing collectively for the first time in its history
insights.locator: TRIPLE TROUBLE / 'The case for doing so'

insights.claim: The UK's predicament most resembles 1976, when Callaghan's Labour government took an IMF bailout and was forced by markets to abandon its postwar Keynesian strategy, then spent 18 years out of power for accepting that discipline.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: A precedent suggesting market discipline will eventually force a painful reckoning regardless of the governing party's preferences.
insights.locator: TRIPLE TROUBLE / 'The situation today most closely resembles that of 1976'

insights.claim: Matthijs's earlier prediction (2017 and 2024 Foreign Affairs essays) that Brexit would damage the UK more than the EU and eventually force a reckoning has been borne out, with the EU growing more cohesive through crises while Brexit became a continental cautionary tale.
insights.speaker: Matthias Matthijs
insights.so_what: Reinforces the author's track record and the directional view that UK isolation, not EU fragmentation, is the realized outcome.
insights.quote: The United Kingdom has learned the hard way that smallness in an integrated world is not the same as sovereignty.
insights.locator: THE END OF THE ROAD / 'In an essay published in Foreign Affairs in early 2017'
